observations.recommendation:
1.16 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to (a) direct
the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) regularly monitor and reconcile the
balances of the SL and GL to prevent discrepancies; (ii) ensure the timely
preparation, reconciliation, and submission of monthly BRS to the Audit Team;
(iii) promptly record identified book reconciling items in the accounting
records to ensure the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank and other affected
account balances; and (iv) record necessary adjustments for stale checks by
debiting Cash in Bank and crediting Accounts Payable; and (b) instruct both
the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer to
regularly monitor outstanding checks and ensure that payees are timely
notified of their status, as warranted.

2.12 We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations and the LCE agreed
to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (a) recognize impairment of
receivables in accordance with IPSAS 29; (b) ascertain if conditions are
met for the writing off of receivable accounts, and take appropriate action in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
and (c) coordinate with the PaMES Administrator to determine the causes
of the discrepancies and reconcile the records accordingly.

3.13 We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations and the LCE agreed
to direct (a) all Accountable Officers and employees to ensure liquidation
of cash advances within the prescribed period as required by COA
Circulars No. 97-002; and (b) adopt appropriate and legal remedies to
enforce the settlement of cash advances granted to officials and employees,
as applicable. We also recommend that Management (a) conduct a
thorough review of the subsidiary ledgers to identify and rectify all
incorrectly classified transactions; and (b) reinforce the proper application
of the Revised Chart of Accounts as provided under COA Circular No.
2015-009 to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the Municipality’s
financial records and reports.

4.21 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Municipal
Assessor’s Office, Accounting Office, and Treasurer’s Office to maintain
constant coordination to ensure the regular updating and reconciliation of
their respective records related to RPT and SET transactions; (b)
Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
balances of the RPT and SET receivable accounts, along with their
corresponding liability accounts, as warranted; and (c) Municipal
Treasurer’s Office (i) request/designate a staff that will compute the RPT
and SET for the current and previous years; and (ii) intensify efforts to
enhance the RPT collection rate by implementing more proactive and
strategic collection measures.

5.22 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) Inventory
Committee to conduct the physical count of inventories at least every six
months and prepare the RPCI and RPCSP for the inventories counted; (b)
Municipal Accountant to (i) utilize the perpetual inventory method; (ii)
adopt COA Circular Nos. 2024-004 and 2024-006; (iii) maintain the
necessary records in accounting for inventories; and (iv) coordinate with the
MTO for the regular reconciliation of inventory balances; and (c) GSO to
maintain the required records and reports for inventories.

6.27 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) create an Inventory,
Appraisal, and Disposal Committees; (b) instruct the Inventory Committee
to (i) conduct physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality; (ii)
submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team on or before January 31 of each year;
and (iii) reconcile the inventory reports with the accounting records; (c)
Acting Municipal Treasurer to (i) maintain PC and PAR for the proper
control and monitoring of the LGU’s property and equipment; (ii) ensure that
all vehicles in active use are registered with the LTO and covered with
property insurance; and (iii) submit to the Audit Team copies of OR/CR of
all motor vehicles and heavy equipment for verification of its ownership;
(d) Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELC; (e) Municipal Accountant and
Acting Municipal Treasurer to (i) consider adopting the one-time cleansing
option for PPE accounts; and (ii) in coordination with the accountable
officers, prepare IIRUP to report all unserviceable properties; and (f)
Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable PPEs in
accordance with the prescribed guidelines and procedures.

7.18 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Municipal Engineer
to (i) expedite the retrieval of the Program of Works, technical
specifications, and other relevant documents pertaining to the 23
infrastructure projects that remain recorded under the CIP account; (ii)
prepare and submit Progress Reports or Status of Completion for said
projects to determine their eligibility for reclassification to the appropriate
Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) accounts; and (iii) regularly provide
the OIC–Municipal Accountant with updated Status Reports of Completion,
advance copies of Final Inspection Reports, and duly approved Certificates
of Completion and Acceptance to facilitate the timely reclassification of
completed projects to their respective PPE accounts; and the (b) OIC–
Municipal Accountant to (i) use the Final Inspection Reports, Certificates of
Completion, and Acceptance as the basis for reclassifying completed CIP
projects to their appropriate asset accounts, regardless of the payment status
as of the reporting date; (ii) compute and record the corresponding
depreciation for reclassified CIP accounts in compliance with applicable
accounting standards; (iii) effect the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
identified recording errors; and (iv) exercise due diligence in recording all
transactions to prevent errors and misstatements in the financial records.

8.26 We recommended and the LCE agreed through the implementing and
concerned offices to (a) ensure continued adherence to the terms and
conditions of current and future fund transfers from NGAs to maximize
fund utilization and achieve intended program outcomes; (b) OIC-
Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and record the necessary adjusting
journal entries to correct the abnormal balances under the Due to NGAs
account; and (ii) coordinate with the source agencies to obtain formal
instructions on the appropriate disposition of the unutilized fund transfers,
taking into account the provisions of the respective Memoranda of
Agreement (MOAs); and (c) OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Acting
Municipal Treasurer to (i) request from the depository bank and/or BOTr
detailed information regarding the unidentified credit transactions posted
to the Municipality’s bank account; and (ii) facilitate the return of
unutilized fund balances to the concerned source agencies, accompanied by
the corresponding reports of disbursement, in compliance with Section 6.7
of COA Circular No. 94-013.

9.21 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer
to (a) verify and assess the noted tax deficiencies of the five identified
business taxpayers, and take appropriate actions thereon; (b) align their
licensing procedures with the Revenue Code of the Municipality and
enforce the timely submission of ITR for all business taxpayers regardless
of business size; (c) make representation with the proper office for the
hiring of additional personnel or assignment of staff who will be trained
and assigned in the examination of books of accounts of businesses; and (d)
exercise the authority granted under Section 171 of RA 7160 for more
accurate tax assessments.

10.13 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the HRMO to (a) conduct
a comprehensive study to develop an Organizational Structure and
Staffing Pattern Plan; (b) formulate policies and guidelines to assess the
necessity and propriety of hiring Job Order (JO) and Contract of Service
(CoS) personnel; (c) implement a more robust internal control system for
recruitment and selection processes; (d) established internal policies or
procedures for managing absences, monitoring attendance, and reporting
responsibilities; and (e) ensure that the actual tasks performed by JOs and
CoS personnel are consistent with the duties and responsibilities specified
in their respective contracts.

11.14 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal
Development Council Executive Committee to strengthen the planning of
the PPAs to ensure their timely implementation; (b) BAC, TWG and OIC-
Municipal Accountant to (i) submit all the requisite documents; and (ii)
strictly comply with pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR and
COA Circular No. 2012-001 in all procurements; and (c) OIC-Municipal
Accountant to (i) strengthen her review function on the requisite
documentation of procurements; (ii) facilitate the immediate submission of
the unsubmitted accounts to support the regularity of the subject
disbursements; and (iii) submit all DVs including the complete supporting
documents of each month, on or before the tenth day of the ensuing month.

12.26 We recommended and the LCE agreed through the (a) MDRRM Office to
(i) maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF by ensuring the timely
implementation of programmed PPAs within the budget year; (ii)
regularly review and update the LDRRMFIP to reflect newly identified or
reprogrammed PPAs; (iii) ensure that new or reprogrammed PPAs are
duly supported by corresponding Sangguniang Bayan Resolutions; and (iv)
promptly submit the monthly utilization reports to oversight agencies; and
(b) OIC–Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to
transfer and record the understated amount in the STF, and to ensure the
accurate computation and proper recording of such transactions in
subsequent periods.

13.19 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) sustain and strengthen its
efforts to ensure adequate attribution and efficient utilization of the GAD
fund in support of gender mainstreaming initiatives; (b) formulate a multi-
year GAD Agenda, guided by the templates provided in PCW Memorandum
Circular No. 2018-04 dated September 19, 2018, to serve as the strategic
basis for the preparation of annual GAD Plans and Budgets; and (c) apply the
HGDG tool when attributing costs to GAD PPAs to ensure accurate and
appropriate cost attribution.

14.3 We recommended the LCE agreed that through the Municipal Accounting
and Treasury Offices to sustain the timely withholding and remittance of
taxes in accordance with pertinent regulations.

15.3 We commended Management for the sustained observance of the GSIS Act
of 1997, and advised to continue the withholding and timely remittance of
all premium contributions and loan amortizations of its employees,
government’s share to the fund and the amortization of prior years’
obligations.

16.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed (a) through the Municipal
Accounting and Treasury Offices to ensure the full remittance of the
outstanding balances; (b) make preliminary discussions with the
PhilHealth in the possible restructuring of the Municipality’s unsettled
accounts; (c) to instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to draw journal
entry to correct the year-end balances of Due to PhilHealth account; and
(d) put measures in place to monitor the timely and intact remittances of
withheld premiums to avoid potential penalties and to ensure continuous
benefits for the members.

17.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed (a) through the Municipal
Accounting and Treasury Offices to ensure the full remittance of the
outstanding balances; (b) instruct the Human Resources and Management
Office, in coordination with the Accounting Office, to make representations
with the Pag-IBIG for the facilitation of issuance of Pag-IBIG numbers of
employees; (c) make preliminary discussions with the Pag-IBIG in the
possible restructuring of the Municipality’s unsettled accounts; and (d)
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to draw journal entry to correct the
year-end balance of Due to Pag-IBIG.

18.3 We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) accountable officials
and employees of the Municipality to exercise due prudence in the
disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances;
(b) require the named person liable for the Notice of
Suspensions/Disallowances to comply immediately with the requirements
stated in the said Notice.

We recommended that Management: 1. The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the end-user Departments/Offices, submit to the Municipal Accountant the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued, together with the Requisition and Issue Slip, for the unreconciled inventories of ₱17,862,312.79, as basis of the latter for recognizing the expenses in the books of accounts; and 2. Henceforth, the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal Treasurer conduct a regular reconciliation (e.g. monthly, quarterly) of their respective Inventories records.

We recommended that Management: 1. Management direct the MTO personnel in-charge to furnish the Accounting Office with the Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued for the issuance of semi-expendable properties amounting to ₱6,952,333.29, and the latter derecognize the same in the LGU’s books of accounts; and 2. Henceforth, the MTO personnel in-charge of semi-expendable properties and the Municipal Accounting Office conduct periodic reconciliation of their respective semi-expendable properties records.

We recommended that Management: 1. The Municipal Accountant take up the necessary adjustments reconciling items required during the preparation of monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements to reflect the correct balance of the Cash in Bank accounts; 2. The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant coordinate and facilitate the immediate reconciliation of the unexplained balances derived from closed accounts from various banks; and 3. Henceforth, prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and adjust the books accordingly.

We recommended that Management: 1. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to cancel all unclaimed stale checks, if any, and report the same together with the original DVs and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant for proper accounting thereof, in line with Section 59 of the MNGAS; 2. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send a written notice to the payee of the existence of a check, at least one month before the check becomes stale; and 3. Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements, and make the necessary adjusting journal entries for stale checks, if any.

The Audit Team recommended to the Management the following: 1. The Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting journal entry in order to reconcile the reciprocal accounts and to recognize the liability of the LGU of ₱5,000,000.00; 2. The Municipal Treasurer facilitate the immediate transfer of the NTF-ELCAC Funds amounting ₱5,000,000.00 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund; and 3. Henceforth, the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal Treasurer, coordinate and communicate in timely and effective manner, to the Municipal Accountant the guidelines pertaining to the release, utilization, and implementation of NTF-ELCAC Funds.

We recommended that Management: 1. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer/Acting General Services Officer cause the immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties of the Municipality in accordance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1; and 2. The Municipal Accountant drop from the books of accounts all the properties disposed of on the basis of Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties, Waste Materials Report or Property Transfer Report or their equivalent documents, whichever is/are applicable.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant, reconcile the financial records, ensure the recording of all unrecorded deposits, and make the necessary adjustments to accurately reflect the correct balance of the account.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to: 1. Request the Accountable Officer to submit a written justification as to why his/her collections were not remitted daily and were delayed between seven to 80 calendar days; 2. Discontinue the practice of using collections to disburse financial assistance to AICS beneficiaries, and ensure that all collections are deposited intact in accordance with prescribed regulations; and 3. The Municipal Accountant to verify the discrepancies between the collections recorded in the RCD and OR and the corresponding deposit slips before recording the transactions.

We recommended that Management: 1. The Municipal Treasurer to liquidate her cash advances in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; 2. Management stops the practice of transferring the cash from the accountable officer to the activity proponents; and 3. Management requires the proponent of the Boy PadyakOragon competition to explain the late submission of the liquidating documents to the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that Management: 1. The Municipal Accountant effect the recording of the fund transfer/grant amounting ₱401,910.07 immediately, thereafter, submit to the audit team the JEV of the pertinent transaction; 2. The Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments on dormant balances in the Due to NGAs account that requires verification and/or adjustments; 3. The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and/or implementing department/office, facilitate the immediate liquidation and return of any unutilized fund transfers/grants upon completion of the projects and programs; and 4. Henceforth, the Municipal Treasurer, coordinate and communicate in timely and effective manner, to the a.) Municipal Accountant for the recording of fund transfer/grants; and to the b.) Implementing Office/Department for the proper implementation and utilization of the fund transfer/grants.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant: 1. Substantiate the reported balance of Other Payables account by preparing and maintaining detailed supporting schedules and an aging report; 2. Provide an update on the status of the business tax under protest recorded under Other Payables, including any recent developments or actions taken; and 3. Submit a list of the recipients of the BAC Honorarium, including their names, positions, and the corresponding amounts received.

We recommended that Management: 1. The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant coordinate and facilitate the immediate reconciliation and verification of undocumented/unsubstantiated obligations amounting ₱376,598.47. Thereafter, take necessary adjustments and/or corrections to establish the correct balance of the Accounts Payable. 2. Require the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the accounts which had been outstanding for two years or more and no actual claim, administrative or judicial has been filed. If upon review, the conditions for reversion exist and merit removal from the book of accounts, the same shall be disclosed in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile their respective records on withholding tax and remittance, verify the unremitted taxes of ₱181,495.88, and cause the remittance to the BIR of any unremitted withholding taxes.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant: 1. Submit the detailed schedule of the unremitted amount as previously requested; 2. Facilitate the immediate remittance of any unremitted amounts without valid or justifiable reasons for non-remittance; and 3. Make the necessary adjustments in the Due to PhilHealth and PhilHealth Contributions accounts to reflect the correct balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management: 1. Management submits a written justification to the Audit Team as to why the 22 PPAs were not fully implemented in CY 2024 which resulted in the low utilization of 20 percent LDF; and 2. Henceforth, Management devise a strategy to facilitate the immediate implementation of its development projects, e.g. schedule the procurement and implementation of the projects throughout the year, and consider the conduct of Early Procurement Activities (GPPB Resolution No. 14-2019) for projects to be implemented in the succeeding year.

We recommended that Management: 1. The MPDC and the Bids and Awards Committee submit a written justification to the Audit Team why the infrastructure projects amounting to ₱7,546,064.50 were implemented by Administration without the necessary revisions of the Annual Procurement Plan; and 2. We recommend that management prioritize filling up the vacant position of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer (MSWDO), and Municipal Assessor (MA) by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving employees for the more effective administration of the said offices which in turn will benefit the municipality as a whole.

We recommend that management maximize the utilization of the 70% component of the LDRRMF to strengthen the municipality’s capacity to build disaster-resilient communities and to enhance disaster preparedness at all levels. Furthermore, submit a justification for the non-preparation of LDRRMFIP to avoid suspension of the DRRM transactions of CY 2022. Ensure compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 which requires preparation of LDRRMFIP in LDRRMF Current Year’s Appropriation and unexpended balance of LDRRMF from the previous year’s transfer to Special Trust Fund.

We recommend that management require the LDRRM Officer to promptly submit the Monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommend management to instruct Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer to coordinate closely in planning and prioritizing the projects in the AIP funded under the 20% Development Fund to maximize the utilization of the fund.
We recommend that management maximize its effort and take steps/procedures to ensure that the LCPC fund may be fully utilized to its programs, projects and activities.

We recommend that management prepare and submit the required AAPSI to the Office of the Auditor within the deadline set, which is within 60 days upon receipt of the Annual Audit Report, following the correct format and incorporating therein all audit findings and recommendations stated in Part II of the said report.

We recommend that management:
Adhere to the rules and regulations of the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular 97-002 and Section 89 of PD 1445.

Send demand letters to concerned officers to enforce immediate settlement of their cash advances otherwise enforce appropriate administrative/legal sanctions.

For the rehired officer as Executive Assistant we recommend to withhold the salary and/ or other benefits due her notwithstanding the imposition of other sanctions provided under Article 217 and 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We recommend management require the Municipal Accountant to:

Stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advance.

Issue demand letters to the concerned officials and employees to settle their unliquidated cash advances. If the settlement was not received, withhold their salaries for those officers/employees who are still employed. For employees who are already resigned and transferred, cause for immediate liquidation or settlement of the unliquidated cash advances. 

Ensure the completeness of prescribed supporting documents for granting cash advances in processing claims for payment thereof, particularly the certification that he/she has no outstanding cash advance.

Request for the write-off of the unliquidated cash advance balances of dead employees.

We recommend that management require the Inventory Committee to prioritize and complete the physical inventory within a reasonable period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. Also, management is enjoined to ensure the full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in the same circular.

We recommend that Accounting Office continue tracing and locating relevant documents to serve as a basis in analyzing prior years’ reconciling items and prepare necessary adjustments. Further, conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to eliminate the difference.

We recommend that management adopt and implement the guidelines for the use of post-paid plan subscriptions taking into consideration the following:

Require the accounting office to include in the DV particulars the end-user of the post-paid mobile plans and ensure that the concerned officials and employees submit the billing statements to the accounting office, including the breakdown of additional charges incurred before processing the vouchers.

Grant only to officials and employees who are department heads and consider the necessity, amount and rank in the ceiling of the postpaid mobile plan.

Stop allowing the payment of gadget cash-out or device amortization to the monthly subscription plan. Request the end-user to refund of the gadget cash-out or device amortization on the previous billing statement.

Utilize alternative modes of communication such as the internet for long-distance communication or consider the features of the communication network of its unlimited calls for Smart to Smart/Globe to Globe phones that provide cost-saving measures.

We recommend the Municipal Treasurer renew the registration of all service motor vehicles with the LTO in compliance with RA No. 4136 to avoid penalties for late payment and payment of damages for third-party liabilities in case of accidents.

We recommend that management require the MPDC and Municipal Engineer to closely monitor and coordinate in fast tracking the implementation of the programs, projects and activities under the 20 percent Development Fund in the year the funds were appropriated in order to ensure that the intended beneficiaries could avail of the maximum benefits and desired outcome from the said program or projects.

We recommend that management closely monitor and ensure that the LDRRM planned program/projects/activities are implemented and give due regard on the maximum utilization of the 70% for pre disaster preparedness programs so that the intended purpose is attained/achieved as required in Section 1 and 2 of Rule 18, Republic Act 10121.

We recommend that management ensure and fast track the implementation of the programs, projects and activities under the 1% LCPC fund as required under Section 15 of RA No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to ensure the achievement of its intended purpose.

We recommend that management prioritize filling up the vacant position of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer (MSWDO), and Municipal Assessor (MA) by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving employees for the more effective administration of the said offices which in turn will benefit the municipality as a whole.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the recipient barangays for the immediate submission of the FUR. Upon receipt of the FUR, instruct the Engineering Office to conduct inspection and validate the reported completed projects. Further, prepare adjusting entries after validation and verification of the FUR.

We recommend the Municipal Assessor to prioritize the registration and transfer of title of the land to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in compliance with Section 39 of PD No.1445. Also, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor have to reconcile their records of the land owned by the LGU.

We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer remit/release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax and community tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in consonance with the provisions of Section 271 of RA 7160 of the Local Government Code of 1991 for the barangays to have additional fund to support its Programs, Projects and Activities which are beneficial to its constituents.

We recommend that management create a GAD Focal Point System to ascertain the alignment of GAD agenda and propose activities identified as gender issues. Also, we recommend the establishment of GAD database/sex-disaggregated data to come up with an evidenced-base programs, activities/projects intended for gender causes. Moreover, maximize the utilization of GAD funds in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided.

We recommend to the BAC to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) in the prescribed format and submit the same to the GPPB within fourteen (14) calendar days after the end of the semester as required under Section 12.2 of RA 9184 and its IRR to enable the GPPB to perform its duty, among others, of evaluating the effectiveness of the government procurement system and recommending improvements in its system and procedures.
We recommend that management observe the guiding principles as prescribed under the Local Government Code and the DILG-DOF Joint Memorandum Circulars to formulate an updated Local Revenue Code.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant update and make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the general/subsidiary ledgers and to reconcile regularly with the records of the disbursing officer. Assign another employee to make the reconciliation if the Municipal Accountant is already overloaded with her work.
The LDRRMC must come up with a program of work for the efficient utilization of the disaster funds giving due regard on the timely assistance in their lowliest times of the earthquake victims.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive require the Municipal General Services Office (GSO)-Designate to complete the physical inventory of the Municipality’s PPE taking into consideration the prescribed procedural guidelines for inventory taking;
The Municipal GSO-Designate segregate those semi-expendable properties with cost below the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 from the RPCPPE and prepare a Report on Semi-Expendable Properties; and
The Municipal GSO-Designate prepare and reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting records and furnish the Audit Team with the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive mandate the Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO designate to promptly and meticulously post the acquisition of PPE to the respective ledger cards and property cards upon receipt of the source documents and keep and maintain the same in accordance with the provision of the NGAS Manual; and
The Management conduct a one-time cleansing of the Municipality’s PPE and strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the accurate balance of Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Time Deposits and Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account; and
The Municipal Accountant, in the succeeding reporting period, recognize investment in time deposit with maturity of 90 days as cash and cash equivalent for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Enforce strictly the full settlement of all unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2023;
Prioritize issuance of demand letters to all accountable officers who are still in active service;
Impose the withholding of salary and the institution of other sanctions as provided under paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
Request for write-off the cash advances which have remained in active or non-moving in the books for 10 years or more and where settlement/collectivity could no longer be ascertained pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Management Comment:
Management commented that they have already issued demand letters to accountable officers who are still active in service and they are in the process of preparing the request for write-off of cash advances which have remained inactive or non-moving in the books for 10 years or more.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accounting Office conduct reconciliation of Due to NGAs account against relevant records and effect proper adjustments for deficiencies noted and errors uncovered to reflect the correct account balance;
Likewise, the Municipal Accounting Office maintain subsidiary records for all funds received on a per project basis; and
The Local Chief Executive direct the concerned Department Head to coordinate among themselves for keeping of essential records on fund transfers from NGAs such as the Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit copies of all perfected government contracts and all other supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor with five working days from the date of perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to facilitate timely auditorial and technical review thereof.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure the complete transfer of 20 percent NTA from GF to 20% DF account for the full implementation of all priority development projects so that benefits therefrom could be timely received by the constituents and optimum utilization of all its resources could have been attained at its programmed cost avoiding added expenses due to increase in prices of materials and cost of labor.
We further recommended and the Management agreed to conduct reevaluation on the necessity of the identified priority project which to date, remained unimplemented and cause the reversion or re-alignment of the appropriated funds for Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) no longer needed or necessary so that same may be used effectively and efficiently for more appropriate priority development projects.
Management Comment:
Management commented that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer had effected the transfer of funds on February 13, 2024 representing prior year 20% DF cash and fund availability requirement. The Local Finance Committee agreed with the Office of the Municipal Engineer to review the local developments projects under continuing appropriation program. Those projects which were completely implemented will now be delisted to come up with the final and clean list. With the foregoing reconciliation, the difference between actual and estimated national tax allotment in prior years and current years will be considered. Knowing that this may contribute to the funds availability, the local finance committee will be prudent enough to allocate funds or provide supplementation if necessary undergoing correct budget process.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accounting and Budget Offices establish the accurate balance of the STF-DRRM by recording only those unexpended balance from QRF and DRRMF-MOOE and  the unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF for Capital Outlay shall be made continuing in the General Fund books until the projects funded therefore are completed pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
Henceforth, disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statement and accounting record the particular year to which the balances of STF-DRRM were unexpended for proper monitoring and budgeting and any unutilized balance aged more than five years shall be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the GAD Focal Person to expedite the setting up and maintenance of the GAD database to appropriately identify gender issues and provide basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and
Direct the Monitoring and Evaluation Team to conduct annual monitoring of the accomplishments and evaluation of the utilization and outcome of the GAD budget and prepare the form prescribed in Item H of the JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive ensure the timely submission of properly accomplished action plan on the CY 2023 AAR findings and recommendations including that of the prior years and submit the same to the audit team; and
Hereafter, the Management shall faithfully observe the 60-day period within which to submit to the audit team from receipt of the succeeding AARs, either in printed form or by way of electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit findings and recommendations.

We recommended and management agreed to prioritize and ensure the implementation of the “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 by establishing an Inventory Committee comprising an adequate number of committed personnel tasked with spearheading its implementation, with the coordination of the Property Unit/Custodian and Municipal Accountant who shall provide with updated and reconciled balances of the PPE in their respective records.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant trace, analyze, and reconcile the reciprocal accounts - Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances which will be furnished to the Audit Team. We further recommended and management also agreed for the preparation and maintenance of the necessary subsidiary records supporting the balances of the said reciprocal accounts to aid in the reconciliation at year-end.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant verify and analyze the factors contributing the occurrence of the negative balances. If appropriate, adjustments shall be done in the books to effect the necessary corrections, submit the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Commission on Audit with support of the proper documents.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all depreciable PPEs are subjected to depreciation thereby presenting the asset accounts at their correct carrying amounts.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the factors contributing to the occurrence of the noted unreconciled difference in the Cash-Local Treasury and Cash in Bank-LCCA accounts and effect proper adjustments in both records. Further, we recommended and management also agreed that both Department Heads reconcile their cash records at least quarterly to ensure reliability and accuracy of the balances recorded.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer and identify the status of the missing disbursement vouchers and supporting documents. For checks already released, the DV and supporting documents together with the RCI shall be forwarded to the Accounting Office; if not, the related liability account shall be set-up instead of deducting the amount outright from the Cash-In-Bank account. Further, we recommend that, henceforth, the process in the disbursements of checks in accordance with Section 43 and 44 of the MNGAS Manual for LGUs be followed.
The Municipal Accountant has informed us that certain DVs and the accompanying documents have been forwarded by the Municipal Treasurer. These, including their corresponding JEV, will subsequently be submitted to the Audit Team for thorough verification.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive develop and issue an internal policy that would ensure compliance by the Municipal Treasurer on the deposit of collections in accordance with Section 32 of the MNGAS, Vol I.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately submit to the Accounting Office the liquidation of any outstanding balances. Furthermore, we recommended and management agreed that a separate officer will be designated to be a Special Disbursing Officer who will be responsible for all cash advances for special purposes or activities to strengthen the agency's internal controls and to prevent the possible joggling and misapplication of funds.
Additionally, the Municipal Accountant shall forward the Report of Disbursements along with all supporting documents, submitted by the Municipal Treasurer for the liquidation of the mentioned cash advances, for our review and verification.
The Municipal Treasurer is currently in the process of finalizing all liquidation reports. As per the Municipal Accountant, preliminary liquidation reports will soon be submitted to the Audit Team for review and verification, along with the initial email of JEVs.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant implement controls to ensure that all required supporting documentation is attached before authorizing cash advance transactions, aligning with COA Circular 97-002, including the application for the bonding requirement of all accountable officers in accordance to Section 305 (f) of RA 7160 and Item 4.1.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

We recommend and management agreed that the LDRRM Officer prepare and submit a revised LDRRMFIP in lieu of the Work and Financial Plan, which shall be integrated in a revised AIP approved by the local sanggunian and LCE with the recommendation also of the LDRRMC to support the various expenditures charged to the Unspent LDRRMF of the prior years in consonance to Section 6.5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-01, dated March 25, 2013 and Sections 5.1.2 and 5.1.15 of COA Circular 2012-002, dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended and management agreed to discontinue granting advance payment on 15% mobilization cost on contracts entered into unless such requests are duly supported with an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent amount from a commercial bank, bank guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand issued by a surety or an insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission pursuant to Annex E of the Updated 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended the management to return all unused balances of fund transfers to the SAs to comply with the provisions stated in Item 6.7 of COA Circular 94-013, dated December 13, 1994.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Vice-Mayor, acting on behalf of the Municipal Mayor, proposed consolidating the remaining balances designated for the same purpose. The suggestion is to reappropriate these funds for projects aligned with their original intended purpose.
If the respective Memorandum of Agreement (MOA)/Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) for the involved projects stipulates that any remaining balances are not to be returned to the source agency and can be utilized by the implementing agency, then the LGU, through its Local Council, can proceed with the proposed reappropriation. However, if the MOA/MOU does not contain such provision, the default procedure, as outlined in Item 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013, mandates returning the remaining balances to their source agencies through the Bureau of Treasury. Should management intend to use it for other projects, they shall first secure a written consent from the respective source agencies.

disbursement vouchers for payment.

documents. The latter also commented that they will henceforth require the supporting

prescribed in section 6.1.5 COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular

observations.recommendation:
With the foregoing, we recommended and the management agreed on the
following:
1. For the Municipal General Service Officer to:
a. intensify effort to complete the physical count of all PPEs as well as collaborate
with the Municipal Accountant to investigate and reconcile the discrepancies
noted between the report on physical count of PPE and the book balances;
b. observe the annual submission of the physical inventory reports on PPEs to the
Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to existing
rules and regulations for timely review and validation;
c. maintain an updated property cards for each PPE in order to adequately account
the assets of the Municipality;
d. conduct disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with the
guidelines set in the HSPMS; and
e. submit the necessary documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant,
after the disposal, as basis of the latter in dropping the PPEs in the books of
accounts.

2. For the Municipal Accountant to:
a. collaborate with the Municipal GSO to develop a plan in order to reconcile the
noted discrepancies between the book balances and report on physical count of
PPE as well as assist the GSO in completing the physical count thereof;
b. analyze reconciling items and draw journal entries to accurately reflect the
balances of the involved accounts;
c. henceforth, coordinate with the Budget Office as far as practicable in
identification of proper accounts during budgeting, and ensure recognition of
PPE items under their respective appropriate account to foster fairness in the
reporting of individual account balances; and
d. draw journal entry vouchers after the disposal to derecognize the properties and
effect adjustments in the books accordingly.

3. For the Local Chief Executive to evaluate the need to augment manpower capacity
of the Municipal General Services Office, and make necessary action thereon.

We recommended and the Municipal General Service Officer agreed on the
following:
a. Expedite the processing of transfer of titles of pertinent LGU land parcels to its
name, in order to ensure its legal ownership and completely protect its interest, in
compliance with applicable rules and regulations.
b. After registering the real properties in the LGU’s name, notify the Commission on
Audit (COA) thereof in accordance with Article 47 of the Local Government Code.

With the foregoing, we recommended and the management agreed on the
following:
1. For the Municipal General Service Officer to:
a. prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network;
b. maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its
components; and
c. maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map.

2. For the Municipal Accountant to:
a. prepare and maintain Local Road Network Ledger Cards for the road and its
components namely: road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection
structures and other miscellaneous structure;
b. prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation of each road
component; and
c. disclose accordingly the Road Network System in the Notes to Financial
Statements.

3. For the Municipal Engineer to:
a. assist the Municipal GSO in conducting the physical inventory of the Local
Road Network in order to come up with the Report on the Physical Count of
Local Road Network; and
b. provide Municipal Accountant and the Municipal GSO complete description
and cost segregation of road components of every road project undertaken, for
them to have a basis in recording the road networks in the Local Road Network
Ledger Card and Local Road Network Property Card, respectively.

4. For the Municipal Assessor to help in determining the costing of the land where the
local road networks are erected.

Therefore, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile the
balances of the CIP accounts with the pertinent records of Engineering and other offices,
to determine the status of remaining balances and immediately transfer all completed PPAs
to proper PPE accounts and accordingly record depreciation therefor.
We, also, recommended and the Municipal Engineering Office agreed to provide
periodically the Accounting Office of the implementation status of infrastructure projects
undertaken by the LGU to assist in the periodic review and monitoring of the pertinent CIP
balances thereof. The status report must be supported with Certificate of Completion on
completed PPAs and Statement of Work Accomplished on on-going PPAs.

To address the above observations, we recommended and the Municipal
Accountant agreed on the following:
a. prioritize coordination with the concerned national agencies, through formal
correspondence as regards the dormant balances under Due to Other NGAs
Account;
b. intensify effort to locate documents to substantiate the dormant accounts, among
other legal means of verifying existence of valid obligation; and
c. thereafter, determine applicability of Section 98 of PD 1445, particularly on those
accounts which remained outstanding for more than two years.

Therefore, we reiterated our previous recommendations and the management
agreed that the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal Accountant, Municipal
Treasurer, BAC Chairman and other personnel concerned to:
a. discontinue/stop the practice of procuring goods through cash advances;
b. conduct the procurement activities in accordance with the provisions in the Revised
IRR of RA No. 9184 and guidelines provided by GPPB resolutions, specifically
during state of calamity;
c. ensure that required documents are attached to the liquidation reports of cash
advances before certifying that the cash advances are liquidated for proper
evaluation of the transactions; and
d. be steadfast in observing all laws, rules and regulations pertaining to procurement,
utilization of 20% LDF and other covid-19 funds.

With the foregoing, we recommended and the management agreed on the
following:
1. The Sangguniang Bayan, as assisted by the Municipal Budget, to ensure henceforth,
that amendments in DRRM-PPAs are adequately supported with LDRRMFIP or
Supplemental thereof, before enactment of budget therefor.
2. The MDRRMO, in corroboration to his claim on the pertinent lacking supplemental
LDRRMFIP, to submit the same to this office for evaluation, and ensure hereafter
that all prospective changes in DRRM-PPAs are supported with LDRRMFIP or
supplemental thereof.

We recommended and the MDRRMO agreed on the following:
a. in coordination with the MAO, to steadfastly monitor the status and practicability
of completion or implementation of DRRM-PPAs, funded by current year 5%
DRRMF, STF and Continuing Appropriation, with the view of identifying savings
from completed or unviable projects; and
b. recommend promptly and accordingly to the MDRRMC and MBO the identified
savings, to aid them on their evaluation of available funding sources during budget
preparation for LGU priority PPAs.

To address the above observations, we recommended and the Municipal
Accountant agreed on following:
a. coordinate accordingly with the HDMF and GSIS offices with regard to the
discrepancies in the remittances of loan amortizations, to settle the gaps noted.
Thereafter, reconcile and update the agency records, if necessary; and
b. ensure that necessary improvement in the payroll processing will be implemented
as soon as possible, particularly in the monitoring of employee loan balances and
corresponding amortization payment, to foster reconciliation of loan accounts and
henceforth avoid over-withholding of salaries.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed on the following:
1. The Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Municipal Development
Council (CDC) and the Local Finance Committee (LFC), and in consideration of
the limited manpower available to implement 20% LDF PPAs promptly, consider
in the planning and budgeting phases, prioritization of relevant and feasible PPAs,
strategically develop mechanisms to optimize utilization of the fund and realization
of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes
2. Municipal Planning Development Coordinator;
a. conduct a thorough planning for the implementation of developmental projects
by considering the appropriate timing and phasing of their implementation,
among others;
b. coordinate with the concerned offices, such as the Engineering Department,
among others, particularly with regard to PPAs to be included in the 20% LDF
AIP, to determine the specific works to be done thereon so as to consider
logistics optimally such as the assignment of equipment, thus avoid delays in
the implementation; and
c. strategize monitoring of all PPAs and preparation of pertinent reports by
optimizing available manpower capacity.
3. the Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairman, to reconsider implementation of some
projects “by contract” through qualified contractors, so that more PPAs will be
implemented as scheduled.

With the foregoing, we recommended and the MDRRMO agreed on the following:
a. coordinate with the concerned offices, such as the Engineering Department,
among others, particularly with regard to PPAs to be included in the LDRRMFIP,
to determine the specific works to be done thereon, so as to consider logistics
optimally such as the assignment of equipment, thus avoid delays in the
implementation; and
b. as the secretariat of the MDRRMC, and mainly the one designated to propose
DRRM-PPAs and monitor status of its implementation, to prioritize in the
planning and implementation, more viable PPAs related to disaster prevention
and preparedness, besides the 30% lump-sum allocation to disaster-response, and
ensure that the LDRRMF are optimally utilized in accordance with RA 10121.

Direct the LDRRM Council to decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years so that the Municipality becomes a disaster resilient community.

Instruct the General Services Office to conduct physical count of inventories on a semestral basis to support the balance of accounts in the Financial Statements and reconcile its records with the Municipal Accounting Office for fair presentation of the relevant accounts in the Financial Statements.

Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to file a request for write-off of the receivable account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected in accordance to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

The Municipal Treasurer submits to the Municipal Accountant all disbursement vouchers relative to the unrecorded disbursements/encashment; and
Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to locate the documents supporting the reconciling items, make representation with the bank for the furnishing of the original copy of debit and credit advices, and record the reconciling items and the stale checks in the books of accounts.

Municipal Accounting Office exhaust all possible means to identify the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to effect immediate adjustment in the books of accounts.  Likewise, periodic reconciliation of the accounts should be made, to ensure reliability and accuracy of the recorded financial transactions.

Management encourage the JO and contractual personnel to register as SSS self-employed members in adherence to the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-83 in order to avail of the benefits and protection against hazards and disability, sickness, maternity, old age, death and other contingencies that the SSS offers.

Therefore, we recommended and management agreed to do the following to ensure proper control and presentation of accounts in the Financial Statements:
For the Municipal Treasurer to:
● Demand liquidation / remittance of all collections made by the collectors as at the related income year-end with validation of the unused official receipts vis-à-vis collections declared
● Instruct the personnel in-charge of the preparation of the RCDs to update the preparation of the RCD especially as at year-end and submit the same to the accounting office before the closing of the books
For the Municipal Accountant to:
● Prepare the adjusting entries to correct the net misstatement of the income accounts of CY 2020 by considering the collections of CY 2020 that were recorded in CY 2021 to present fairly the affected accounts in the succeeding years.
● Henceforth, instruct the accounting personnel concerned to record all collections relating to all other taxes, fees, charges and other revenues in a cash basis of accounting pursuant to GAM Volume I.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following:
For the Municipal Treasurer:
● Ensure issuance of Official Receipts upon receipt of any collections even if it is an internal transaction of the Municipality such as fund transfers
For the Municipal Accountant
● Ensure that all disbursement vouchers pertaining to fund transfers between Municipality’s funds are duly supported with official receipts.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following to present fairly the receivable accounts of the Municipality in the Financial Statements:
For the Municipal Treasurer to:
● Demand liquidation / remittance of all collections made by the collectors as at the related income year-end with validation of the unused official receipts vis-à-vis collections declared
● Instruct the personnel in-charge of the preparation of the RCDs to update the preparation of the RCD especially as at year-end and submit the same to the accounting office before the closing of the books
For the Municipal Accountant to:
● Conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts and other receivable accounts to ensure that these are collected when these become due and demandable and that cash advances are liquidated within the prescribed period depending upon their nature and purpose
● Exert effort to demand immediately the liquidation of all outstanding cash and advances from the concerned officials/employees who are still connected with the Municipality
● Impose sanctions prescribed in item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 1997-0021 for non-compliance of liquidating cash advances, such as withholding the salaries and other emoluments of the officials/employees who continuously disregard, fail or decline to comply with the demand, including filing appropriate charges against them
● Send demand letters to officials and employees who are no longer connected with the Municipality but whose whereabouts are still known to liquidate their cash advances/receivables and consider taking legal actions/remedy against them.
● Gather all documents that will prove that liquidation/payment of the named individuals is no longer possible such as those who are already deceased (e.g. death certificate) and whose last address is not known (e.g. retirement certification, clearances, etc.) and request for the write off of the receivable for fair presentation of the account in the Financial Statements taking into consideration the liability of those individuals who granted the clearances. Exclude for request for write-off individuals whose receivable arise from disallowances and charges, cash shortages and subject of a pending case in court or before investigative authorities.
● Make representation with the ICFAU to fast tract the issuance of the credit notice, if warranted, to close the long outstanding advances to special disbursing officer account
● Seek legal assistance to ensure collection of the receivable from the absconding Municipal Treasurer
observations.reason:
Lack of necessary documents in other receivable accounts.
Cash Advances granted during the year were already liquidated.
Waiting for credit notice.
Overlooked seeking of assistance from the Legal Office of the Provincial Government of South Cotabato

observations.recommendation:
Therefore, in view of the above observation/gaps noted, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following;
a. Adjust the remaining payable accounts if proven to be undocumented and without existing claimants especially those accounts which are outstanding for more than 2 years as stated in Section 98 of PD 1445
b. Review the validity of accounts payable in a regular basis to promptly determine and establish which of the accounts are already dormant and to take necessary remedial measures
c. Investigate the negative balances to make the necessary demand on the receivable of the management against the concerned supplier/creditor/employee, if applicable
d. Ensure to recalculate and review all payments made to payees before and approving the voucher to minimize overpayments.

Therefore, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant to present the current portion of long-term loans as of year-end as Loans Payable-Current in the financial statements in compliance with Section 367 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs Volume I, for purposes of fair and proper presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

Therefore, in view of observation/gaps noted, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following:
For the Municipal General Service Officer to;
a. Develop, formulate, and implement strategies, policies, and procedures in the conduct of complete and reconciliation of Physical Inventory of PPEs reported for easier identification. Use the prescribed format of the Inventory Report specified in the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs; and
b. Provide complete information on the inventory report such as property number and value, location and status of the property on all the PPEs reported for easy identification.
For the Municipal Accountant to;
a. Coordinate with the GSO and the inventory team, after the conduct of complete inventory count, for the reconciliation of the PPE with the books and the inventory report and make the necessary adjustments in the books.
b. Reconstruct the PPEs with noted “for reclassification” so that proper amounts will be presented in the Financial Statements; and
c. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer on the remaining CIP accounts and transfer all completed PPAs to proper PPE accounts in consideration with the certificate of completion, inspection report and other necessary documents from the Municipal Engineering Office;
d. Draw journal entry vouchers, if proper disposal has been made, to derecognize the disposed properties and apply appropriate accounting procedures to effect adjustments in the books.
For the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Augment manpower needs of the General Service Officer, if budget permits, to ensure immediate conduct of the physical count.
b. Create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to conduct inventory of unserviceable properties and expedite its disposal to minimize the risk of theft and maximize the storage facility.
Finally, require the proper turn-over of documents from the outgoing to the incoming General Services Officer (GSO).
observations.reason:
Reconciliation is still ongoing.
Also, inventory report and unserviceable properties actions are on-going; some documents/activities not yet completed.
Partially implemented due to lack of manpower and system error on the computation of depreciation.
Reclassification and adjustments are on-going; lack of manpower to complete reclassification and physical count.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, BAC Chairman and other personnel concerned to comply with existing provisions as regards the realignment of expenditures to ensure that legislation enacted is sufficient and appropriate in accordance with the provision of law.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following to enhance utilization of the 20% Development Fund:
For the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to:
● Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively and implement the projects and prioritize what should be accomplished for its timely use without sacrificing the quality of work;
● Look into the unimplemented development projects under the 20% Development Fund and determine causes of non-implementation. Formulate a timetable that will determine the exact period when the project will be implemented and monitor the status of implementation of the PPAs, and determine the assigned equipment for timely implementation;
● Provide complete data on the monitoring report as to percentage status, period of implementation and total cost incurred. Reconcile with the Accounting and Engineering Office so that proper amount and status of the project will be presented in the report.
For the Municipal Engineer to:
● Consider implementing some of the projects “by contract” to the qualified contractors so that numerous PPAs will be implemented as scheduled.
● Coordinate with the MPDO and Accounting Office with regards to the total cost incurred of the project so that correct data will reflect on the submitted report.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed to do the following:
● Closely monitor the undertaking of proposed projects and funded out of and LDRRMF to avoid/minimize disaster projected and unexpected risks, and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.
● Conduct review on a quarterly basis on the status of implementation of PPAs, and communicate with the implementing office on their assigned PPAs that are not yet utilized for timely implementation.
● Recommend for the realignment of PPAs that are seen to be not viable for implementation to optimally use the DRRM Funds to circumvent disaster risks.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed that the Local Chief Executive will do the following:
● Instruct the Local Finance Committee to employ trending analysis of data to facilitate realization of the targeted budget and ensure proper management of financial affairs of the Municipality.
● For the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to regularly monitor and evaluate the status of cash availability in relation to restricted cash balances such as continuing appropriation, obligations to be settled and unobligated allotments to prevent further occurrence of cash deficits.
● For the Municipal Treasurer to analyze collection shortfalls/deficiencies to identify and implement revenue enhancing strategies to further improve collection efficiency.
● For the accountable officers to practice restraints and prudence in the disbursement of funds.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer will do the following:
● Refrain from disbursing funds without cash back up
● Prepare a separate cashbook for each special account maintained under the General Fund and ensure proper monitoring and control of such accounts
● Reconcile consolidated subsidiary ledger of Cash in Bank accounts against the individual accounts of General Fund and Special Accounts.
We further recommend for the Municipal Mayor to prioritize the provision for available government vehicle to be utilized by the Municipal Treasurer in depositing collections on time.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Office to:
● Consolidate all the PPAs implemented by the Municipality to ensure the inclusion of all the on-going as well all the projects that are to be implemented and are yet to be implemented for the reporting year in consideration with the actual amounts incurred to warrant transparency and provide auditable information that could be verified by all stakeholders of the LGU.
● Reconcile the amounts incurred by the MPDC with the records of the Accounting Office.

Therefore, we recommended:
1. Require the Local School Board to evaluate and carefully select PPAs to be funded out of the SEF in accordance with the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series 2017
2. Coordinate with the Provincial School Division Superintendent (PSDS) and School Division Superintendent (SDS) for their recommendations as well as priority PPAs to be included in the Annual SEF budget.
3. Stop charging expenditures not related to the purpose of the SEF.
4. Strictly comply with the provisions under Section 100 and 272 of RA 7160.

1. Create an Inventory Team to conduct inventory of Local Roads in the presence of COA Auditor. Take up the road networks as part of Property, Plant and Equipment after performing the accounting and reconciliation procedures.
2. Require the Local Accountant, General Services Office and Municipal Engineer to coordinate and perform the duties enumerated in the COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015 for compliance.
